text: CROOKED ISLANDS, a group situated 23 m. E of Long Island in the Bahamas, on a coral bank between the parallels of 22° 5' and 22° 50' N lat. It comprises a number of small keys and rocky islets, and the three large islands of Crooked Island, Acklin, Fortune, and Castle islands. The first of these is about 20 m. in length on its N side, measuring from WNW to ESE; but its W side is only 9 m. in extent. Bird rock, off the NW point of this island, is in N lat. 22° 51', W long. 74° 22'.—The group of the C. islands present a united surface of 320 sq. m. Pop. in 1842, 804.

CROOKED ISLANDS, a group of small islets and rocks in the gulf of St. Lawrence running out | ESE of Nottingham. Pop. 678. CRO from the N shore, 3 m. to the W of Point Sproule, and 1 m. E of the mouth of Pentecost river.

CROOKED LAKE, a sheet of water in Steuben and Yates cos., in the state of New York, U.S., 18 m. in length, and from 1 to 11 m. wide, which discharges itself into Seneca lake by a stream 7 m. in length, with a fall of 270 ft.—Also a lake in the NW part of Greene co., in the state of Illinois, 9 m. in length by 1 m. in breadth.

CROOKED REACH, that portion of the strait of Magalhaens which is comprised between the W extremity of Ulloa peninsula and the entrance of the Jerome. The promontory of El Morrion, or St. David's head, on this reach, is in S lat. 53° 33' 20", W long. 72° 28′ 31″.

CROOKED RIVER, a township in Ray co., in the state of Missouri, U.S. Pop. 631.—Also a river in the state of Maine, rising in a small lake in Oxford co., and flowing SSE, by a course of 40 m., to Sebago pond in Cumberland co.
CROOKHAM, a tything in the p. of Crondall, Southamptonshire, 4 m. NE by E of Odiham. Pop.

CROOKHAVEN, a village in the p. of Kilmoe, co. Cork, at the head of a bay of the same name, 19 m. WSW of Skibbereen. Pop. 395.—The bay opens 7½ m. NW of Cape Clear island, with a width of 2½ m.; and penetrates the land northward to the extent of 21 m. But C. proper is a narrow inlet from the W side of this bay; strikes off from it at right angles almost immediately within the entrance; and runs 13 m. W to a narrow isthmus which divides it from Barley cove. At its head it has a depth of 18 or 20 ft.; and farther out, an average depth of 20 fath. It offers clean anchorage, and completely landlocked shelter; but, in consequence of the remoteness of its situation, and the rocky rudeness of its shores, it serves little other purpose than that of an asylum from adverse winds. In 1841, a light-house was erected on Rock island, on the N side of the entrance to the bay, in N lat. 51° 28′ 35″, W long. 9° 42′ 31″.

CROOM, a parish and town in co. Limerick. Area of p. 13,437 acres. Pop. 7,097. The surface is part of the rich flat vale of the Maig.—The town of C. is situated on the river Maig, 4 m. SE by S of Adare. Pop. 1,470.
CROOME (EARLS), a parish in Worcestershire, 13 m. NE of Upton-upon-Severn. Area 1,650 acres. Pop. 194.
CROOME - D'ABITOT, or CROMB - OSBORN, a parish in Worcestershire, 44 m. W by S of Pershore. Area 1,510 acres. Pop. 119.
CROOME-HILL, a parish in Worcestershire, 21 m. E of Upton-upon-Severn. Area 840 acres. Pop.
CROPALATI, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria-Citra, 6 m. SW of Rossano. Pop. 1,248.
CROPANI, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria-Ultra 2da, 12 m. ENE of Catanzaro. Pop. 1,115.
CROPEDY, a parish in Oxfordshire, 4 m. N of Banbury. Area 8,460 acres. Pop. 2,765.
CROPSTON, a township in the p. of Thurcaston, Leicestershire, 34 m. SSW of Mount-Sorrel. Pop. 111.
CROPTE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Vergt. Pop. 1,051.
CROPTHORNE, a parish of Worcestershire, 31 m. E by S of Pershore. Area 3,520 acres. Pop. 732.
CROPTON, a township in the p. of Middleton, N. R. of Yorkshire, 41 m. NNW of Pickering. Pop. 335.
CROPWELL-BISHOP, or GREAT CROPHILL, a parish in Nottinghamshire, 8 m. ESE of Nottingham. Area 1,380 acres. Pop. 533.
CROPWELL - BUTLER, or LITTLE CROPHILL, a hamlet in the p. of Tythby, Nottinghamshire, 71 m.

CROSBY, a township in the p. of Cannonby-cross, co. of Cumberland, 3 m. NE by E of Marypool. Pop. 272.—Also a township in the p. of Bottesford, Lincolnshire, 8 m. NW of Glandford-bridge. Pop. 199.—Also a township in Hamilton co., in the state of Ohio, U.S. Pop. 1,876.
CROSBY-UPON-EDEN, a parish in the co. of Cumberland, 3\frac{3}{4} m. NE by E of Carlisle. Area 3,590 acres. Pop. 463.
CROSBY (High), a township in the above p., <math>4\frac{3}{4}</math> m. NE by E of Carlisle. Pop. 146.
CROSBY (Low), a township in the above p., 5 m. E by N of Carlisle. Pop. 133.
CROSBY (North), a township of Upper Canada, in the Johnstown district. Pop. 863.
CROSBY (South), a township of Upper Canada, in the Johnstown district. Pop. 1,003.
CROSBY-GARRET, or GERARD, a township and p. in Westmoreland; 3 m. W by N of Kirby-Stephen. Area 4,580 acres. Pop. 274.
CROSBY (GREAT), a chapelry in the p. of Sefton, Lancashire, 7 m. NW of Liverpool. Area 1,900 acres. Pop. 1,946.
CROSBY (LITTLE), a township in the p. of Sefton, Lancashire, 73 m. NNW of Liverpool. Pop. 394.
CROSBY-RAVENSWORTH, a parish in the co. of Westmoreland, 4 m. N of Orton, in a valley on the river Lyvennet. Area 15,460 acres. Pop. 909.
CROSBYVILLE, a village in Chester district, in South Carolina, U.S., 72 m. N of Columbia.
CROS-DE-CAGNES, a hamlet and coasting-port of France, in the dep. of Var, cant. of Vence.
CROSCOMBE, a parish in Somersetshire, 13 m. WNW of Shepton-Mallet, on the river Brae. Area 1,420 acres. Pop. 804.
CROSE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Creuse, cant. of Felletin. Pop. 1,009.
CROS - DE - GEORAND (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, cant. of Montpezat. Pop. 1,520.
CROSMIE'RES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, cant. of La Flèche. Pop. 1,226.
CROSS, a parish in the island of Sanday in Orkney. Pop. in 1831, 541.

CROSS, a village in the p. of Kilballyowen, co. Clare. Area 14 acres. Pop. 150.—Also a v. in the p. of Balnascreen, co. of Londonderry. It is so closely connected with the v. of Draperstown, in the same p., that the two are sometimes jointly called Draperstown-Cross. Pop. in 1831, 218.—Also a v. in co. Londonderry, on the Faughan rivulet, 4 m. SE of Londonderry.

CROSS (CAPE), a headland on the coast of NW America, to the S of the entrance of Cross sound, in N. lat. 57° 50′, W long. 136° 10′.—Also a cape on the E coast of Florida, in N lat. 46° 27'. CROSS (St.), South Elmham, or Sandcroft, a parish in the co. of Suffolk, 5 m. SSW of Bungay. Area 1,110 acres. Pop. 258.
CROSSAKEEL, a village in the p. of Kilskyre, co. Meath, 5½ m. W of Kells. Pop. 316.
CROSSBOYNE, a parish in co. Mayo, 1 m. S of Claremorris. Area 16,234 acres. Pop. 6,702.
CROSS-CREEK, a township in Jefferson co., in the state of Ohio, U.S., on the Great Miami river. Pop. 1,707.—Also a town in Washington co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 229 m. W of Harrisburg.
CROSSDONEY, a small village in the p. of Kilmore, co. Cavan, 1 m. E of the river Erne.
CROSSEN. See Krossen.
CROSSFELL, a mountain-ridge of Northumberland, in the p. of Kirkhaugh, rising 2,901 ft. above sea-level.
CROSSFORD, a village in the p. of Dunfermline in Fifeshire, 13 m. W of Dunfermline. Pop. 443. CRO
CROSSGAR, a village in the p. of Kilmore, co. Down, 4 m. N by W of Downpatrick. Pop. 675.
CROSSGATES, a village in the parishes of Dunfermline and of Dalgety, in Fifeshire, 31 m. E of Dunfermline. Pop. 646.

CROSSHAVEN, a harbour and a v. in the p. of Templebreedy, co. Cork. The harbour is a creek or small bay on the S side of the estuary of the Annabaoy river. It was the scene of Sir Francis Drake's snug retreat and dexterous manœuvre, when chased into Cork harbour by a superior Spanish fleet. The creek has 3 faths. depth at low water, and is completely landlocked. The v. looks from the shore of the creek toward Cove. Pop. 514.
CROSS ISLAND, an island forming the E side of the entrance of Machias bay, on the coast of Maine, U. S., 3 m. NE \(\frac{1}{2}\) N from the light on the Liby isles.

CROSS KEYS, a village in Gloucester co, in New Jersey, U.S., 46 m. S by W of Trenton.—Also a v. in Rockingham co., in Virginia, 123 m. NW of Richmond.—Also a v. in Union district, in S Carolina, 69 m. NW of Columbia.—Also a v. in De Kalle co., in Georgia, 100 m. NW of Milledgeville.- Also a v. in Macon co., in Alabama, 142 m. SE of Tuscaloosa.—Also a v. in Roane co., in Tennessee, 147 m. ESE of Nashville.

CROSS LAKE, a lake in British North America, intersected by the parallel of 55° 26' N, and the meridian of 108° W, into which Beaver river flows, one of the head-streams of the Missinippi.-Also a lake in the state of New York, U.S., traversed by the Seneca river, 20 m. S of Lake Ontario.
CROSSLAND (South), a township in the p. of Almondbury, 3 m. SSW of Huddersfield, E of the river Colne. Area 1,560 acres. Pop. 2,705.
CROSSMAGLEN, a small market town in the p. of Creggan, co. Armagh, 8 m. NW of Dundalk. Pop. 546.
CROSSMAN ISLAND, one of the Galapagos group, 10 m. S of Duncan island. It is about 8 m. in length, and its S end is in 0° 37' S lat.
CROSSMICHAEL, a parish in the centre of the stewartry of Kirkcudbright. Area 7,696 acres. Pop. 1,321, of which 222 were in the v. of C.

CROSSMOLINA, a parish, containing a town of the same name, in co. Mayo. Area of p. 67,201 acres, of which 3,675 acres are in Lough Conn, and 468 in small lakes. Pop. in 1831, 11,679; in 1841, 12,221. The two highest points are the summits of Bullaunamore and Triestagh mountains, both situated on the S border, and respectively possessing an altitude of 1,274 and 1,067 ft. above sea-level.-The market and post-town of C. stands on the river Deel, 1 m. NW of the head of Lough Conn, 61 m. W of Ballina. Pop. in 1831, 1,481; in 1841, 1,672.
CROSSNA, a village in the p. of Ardcarne, co. Roscommon, 3 m. SW of Keadue.
CROSSPATRICK, a parish, 71 m. W by S of Arklow, partly in co. Wexford and partly in co. Wicklow. Area 1,648 acres. Pop. 1,506.

CROSS-PLAINS, a village in Robertson co., in the state of Tennessee, U.S., 34 m. NW of Nashville.—Also a v. in Ripley co., in Iowa, 78 m. SE of Indianapolis.—Also a v. in Callaway co., in Missour, 38 m. NE of Jefferson.
CROSS RIVER, the largest branch of the Old Calabar river, in Western Africa. It flows from the N for several hundred miles, through a fertile country, of which little is known.
CROSS RIVER, a village in Lewisborough township, in New York, U.S., 172 m. S of Albany.

CROSS ROADS, a village in York co., in Pennsylvania, U. S., 37 m. S of Harrisburg.-Also a v. in Madison co., in Ohio, 53 m. W of Columbus. -Also a v. in Bedford co., in Virginia, 161 m. W by S of Richmond.—Also a v. in Cobb co., in Georgia.—Also a v. in Gasconade co., in Missouri.
CROSSVILLE, a village in Forsyth co., in Georgia, U.S.—Also a v. in Bledsoe co., in Tennessee.
CROSSWICKS, a village in Burlington co., in New Jersey, U.S., 8 m. SSE of Trenton.
CROSTHWAITE AND LYTH, a chapelry in the p. of Haversham, Westmoreland, 41 m. WSW of Kendal. Area 7,880 acres. Pop. 717.

CROSTHWAITE, a parish in Cumberland, 1 m. NW of Keswick, and 10 m. ESE of Cockermouth. Area 58,330 acres. Pop. in 1811, 3,547; in 1831, 4,344; in 1841, 4,759. This extensive parish, comprising the townships of Braithwaite, Coledale, or Portingale, Keswick, and Under-Skiddaw, and the chapelries of Borrowdale, Newland, Thornwaite, St. John's, Castlerigg, and Wythburn, abounds with picturesque and celebrated scenery. It comprehends the universally admired vale of Keswick, with its beautiful villas and hamlets, and the lakes of Derwentwater, Thirlemere, or Leathes-water, and part of Bassenthwaite. The rugged mountains in this vicinity are rich in copper, lead-ores, and plumbago. There are 8 woollen mills within this parish. Area 58,330 acres. Pop. 4,759.
CROSTON, a parish in Lancashire, 6 m. W of Chorley, on the river Yarrow, and bounded W by the Douglas. Area 17,840 acres. Pop. 3,939.
CROSTON, or Cross-Stone, a chapelry in the p. of Halifax, Yorkshire, 10 m. WSW of Halifax, near the York and Manchester railway.
CROSTWICK, a parish in the co. of Norfolk, 3 m. SSW of Coltishall, near a branch of the Bure. Area 550 acres. Pop. 147.
CROSTWIGHT, a parish in the co. of Norfolk, 33 m. E by S of Walsham. Area 710 acres. Pop. 69.

CROTON CREEK, a river of the state of New York, U.S., which takes its rise in Dutchess co., passes through Putnam and Westchester counties, and throws itself into the Hudson, 17 m. W of Bedford, after a total course of 36 m. The city of New York is supplied with water by an aqueduct brought from this river. This great work was commenced in 1837, and finished in 1842. The C. river is dammed 6 m. from its mouth on the Hudson; the dam is 250 ft. long, 70 ft. wide at the bottom, 7 ft. at the top, and 40 ft. high, built of stone and cement. It creates a pond 5 m. long, covering a surface of 400 acres, and elevates the water 40 ft. From the dam the aqueduct proceeds, sometimes tunnelling through solid rocks and crossing valleys by embankments, through the valley of the Hudson 33 m., until it reaches Harlaem river. It is built of stone, brick, and cement, arched over and under, 6 ft. 3 in. wide at bottom; 7 ft. 8 in. at the top of the side-walls, which are 8 ft. 5 in. high; has a descent of 13\frac{1}{4} inches per m., and will discharge 60,000,000 gallons in 24 hours. It crosses Harlaem river on a magnificent bridge, 1,450 ft. long, with 14 piers, 114 ft. above tide-water at the top, and costing nearly 1,000,000 dollars. The receiving reservoir is 38 m. from the C. dam, covers 35 acres, and contains 150,000,000 gallons. The water is conveyed to the distributing reservoir on Murray's hill by iron pipes; it covers 4 acres, is 43 ft. high above the street, and holds 20,000,000 gallons. Thence the water is distributed by about 150 m. of pipe, principally from 6 to 12 inches in diam. This magnificent work was finished at a cost of 13,000,000 dollars, or £2,700,000: less than the aggregate cost of the various London waterworks, and not above 7 years' purchase of their present income.

CROTOY (LE), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Somme, cant. of Rue, on the r. bank of the estuary of the Somme, 13 m. NW of CRO Abbeville. Pop. in 1841, 1,211. It has a small port, and an active transit trade in wine, brandy, corn, oil, fish, salt, glass-ware, timber, &c. The coast abounds with oysters.
CROTTENDORF, a town of Saxony, in the circle of Zwickau, on the river Zschopau. Pop. 2,500.
CROTTES (LES), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Hautes-Alpes, cant. and 2 m. SSW of Embrun, on the l. bank of the Durance. Pop. 1,496.

CROUCH, a river of Essex, which rises to the S of Billericay; flows E by N; and discharges itself into the North sea, 4 m. E of Burnham. At its mouth are productive oyster-beds. The tide flows 13 m. up this river. Its principal tributary is the Broomhill, which is navigable to Rochford, 7 m.

CROUGHTON, a parish of Northamptonshire, 31 m. SW of Brackley. Area 2,620 acres. Pop. 472. —Also a township in the p. of St. Oswald, Cheshire, 41 m. NNE of Chester, near the Mersey canal. Area 310 acres. Pop. 27.

CROUIN, a village of France, in the dep. and on the r. bank of the Charente, cant. and opposite Cognac, and 26 m. W of Angoulême. Pop. 195. It is noted for its brandy. The environs produce large quantities of grain.
CROULIN ISLES, a group off the coast of Rossshire, near the entrance of Loch Carron. The largest is about 1 m. in length.
CROUPTES, or CROUTES, a village of France, in the dep. of the Orne, cant. of Vimoutiers, 16 m. NE of Argentan. Pop. 836. Linen is made here.

CROUY, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Marne, cant. of Lizy, in a fine valley on the l. bank of the canal de l'Ourcq, 14 m. NE of Meaux. Pop. 1,341. It possesses a handsome town-hall situated in a square, and has an ancient castle now used as a prison. It has three annual fairs, and an active trade in grain, hemp, cattle, poultry, &c.—Also a commune in the dep. and near the r. bank of the Aisne, cant. and 11 m. NE of Soissons. Pop. 1,124.
CROUY-EN-THELLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Oise, cant. of Neuilly-en-Thelle, 16 m. W of Senlis. Pop. 415. Cord and buttons of all sorts are extensively manufactured here.
CROUZET (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Doubs, cant. of Amancey. Pop. 294.
CROUZETTE (LA), a village of France, in the dep. of the Tarn, cant. of Roquecourbe, 8 m. NE of Castres. Pop. 1,010.
CROVIE, a small fishing-village on the Moray Firth, in the p. of Gamrie, Banffshire, 6 m. NW of Aberdour, 1 m. from Gardenstown.
CROW, a river of the North-West-Territory, U. S., which falls into the Mississippi, 15 m. NW of Fort St. Anthony.
CROWAN, a parish of Cornwall, 51 m. NNW of Helstone. Area 7,340 acres. Pop. 4,638.
CROWCOMBE, a parish of Somerset, 7 m. NNE of Wiveliscombe. Area 3,220 acres. Pop. 673.
CROWELL, a parish of Oxfordshire, 42 m. E of Tetsworth. Area 1,160 acres. Pop. 169.
CROW-FEATHER RIVER, a river of the North- West-Territory, U.S., which discharges itself into the Mississippi, in N lat. 45° 55'.
CROWFIELD, a parochial chapelry, connected with the p. of Coddenham, 42 m. ENE of Needham. Area 2,280 acres. Pop. 385.

CROWHURST, a parish of Sussex, 21 m. S of Battle, intersected by a small streamlet. Area 2,180 acres. Pop. 326.—Also a parish of Surrey, 31 m. SE of Godstone, W of the Broad-Mead water. Area 2,250 acres. Pop. 350.
CROWLAND, a township of Upper Canada, in the Niagara district. Pop. in 1841, 973, chiefly Canadians.
CROWLE, a parish in Worcestershire, 5 m. SSE of Droitwich. Area 1,640 acres. Pop. 526.
CROWLEY, a township in the p. of Great Budworth, Cheshire, 5 m. N of Norwich. Area 1,440 acres. Pop. 175.
CROWMARSH-GIFFORD, a parish in Oxfordshire, 1 m. E by N of Wallingford. Area 480 acres. Pop. 330.

CROWN-POINT, a township of Essex co., in the state of New York, U. S., 106 m. NE of Albany, bounded on the E by Lake Champlain. It is mountainous towards the W, and is drained by Putnam's creek and other small streams. The soil consists of clay loam. It contains an old fort, celebrated in the French and revolutionary wars. Pop. in 1840, 2,212. The village consisted at the same period of about 15 dwellings.-Also a village of Lake co., in the state of Indiana, in the centre of Robinson's Prairie, 15 m. S of Lake Michigan.
CROWNTHORPE, a parish in the co. of Norfolk, 2½ m. NW of Wymondham, on the river Yare. Area 520 acres. Pop. 111.
CROWTON, a township in the p. of Weaverham, Cheshire, 5\frac{1}{4} m. W of Norwich, and near the Grand Junction railway. Area 1,260 acres. Pop. 454.

CROXALL WITH EDINGHALL, a parish, partly in Staffordshire, and partly in the co. of Derby, 61 m. N of Tamworth, adjoining the Birmingham and Derby railway. Area, including the townships of Cotton and Oakley, 3,160 acres. Pop. 258.
CROXBY, a parish in the co. of Lincoln, 5 m. ESE of Caistor. Area 1,690 acres. Pop. 106.
CROXDALE, a chapelry in the p. of St. Oswald, co. of Durham, 3 m. S of Durham. Pop. 262.
CROXDEN, a parish in Staffordshire, 54 m. NNW of Uttoxeter. Area 2,400 acres. Pop. 268.

CROXTON, a parish in the co. of Cambridge, 45 m. W of Caxton. Area 1,901 acres. Pop. 264.— Also a township in the p. of Middleton, co. of Chester, 1 m. NNW of Middlewich, bounded S and W by the river Dane. Pop. 48.—Also a parish in Lincolnshire, 7 m. NE by E of Glandford-bridge. Area 1,630 acres. Pop. in 1831, 103.—Also a parish in the co. of Norfolk, 2 m. N of Thetford. Area 3,450 acres. Pop. 330.—Also a township in the p. of Eccleshall, Staffordshire, 3\frac{3}{4} m. NW by W of Eccleshall, on the river Sow. Area 3,790 acres. Pop. 887.
CROXTON-KEYRIAL, a parish in Leicestershire, 9 m. NE of Melton-Mowbray. Area 3,900 acres. Pop. 650.
CROXTON (South), a parish in Leicestershire, 8 m. S of Grimston, on a branch of the river Wreak. Area 1,760 acres. Pop. 297.

CROY, a parish, partly in the co. of Inverness and Nairn, 21 m. in length, and at some points reaching 9 m. in breadth. It is watered by the Nairn, and contains a small loch called the Loch of the Clans. Pop. 1,684. In this p. is Culloden moor, where Prince Charles was defeated by the duke of Cumberland.
CROY, an island in the S Pacific, in Kerguelen's group, in S lat. 50° 0', E long. 68° 36'. It is about 7 m. in diam.
CROYA. See CROIA.
CROYDON, or Crowdon with Clapton, a parish in the co. of Cambridge, 6 m. S of Caxton. Area 2,655 acres. Pop. 441.
CROYDON, a township in Sullivan co., in the state of New York, U.S., 48 m. NW of Concord. Pop. 956.

CROYDON, a market-town and p. in Surrey, 10 m. ESE of Kingston, and 9½ m. S of London, pleasantly situated on the edge of Bansted-downs, near the source of the Wandle. The parish includes eight CRO hamlets within its limits, and a considerable part of the waste called Norwood, which was formerly a forest. It has an area of 9,840 acres. Pop. in 1801, 5,743; in 1841, 16,712. The town is large and handsome. It consists chiefly of one long and spacious street. Some of the houses are elegant, and, generally speaking, all of them substantial and well built. An hospital was founded here in the 38th year of Elizabeth, by Whitgift, archb. of Canterbury, named the hospital of the Holy Trinity. The rental of the property belonging to this hospital, in 1836, amounted to £2,028 3s. 4d. The building, called the hospital, contains 33 dwellings, a chapel, &c., besides a school and schoolmaster's house. C. is the principal place of election for the members for East Surrey. The summer assizes are alternately held here and at Guildford.—C. is supposed to have been the ancient Noviomagus. At the Conquest it was bestowed on Lanfranc, archb. of Canterbury, whose successors in the see have been lords-of-the-manor ever since; and here was an archiepiscopal palace, where the archprimate occasionally resided from 1278 to 1757. In 1573, Archb. Parker entertained Queen Elizabeth and all her retinue here for seven days. Having fallen into decay, it was sold in 1780, under the authority of an act of parliament, for £2,500; and soon after it was fitted up as a calico-printing establishment; but has since been abandoned. Addington park, with its noble mansion, at about 3 m. distance, was purchased for £25,000, and annexed to the see of Canterbury as a more suitable residence for the archbishop.—Addiscombe-house, the seat of the first Lord Liverpool, about 1 m. to the NE of C., is now a military college for educating cadets for the service of the East India company. There are 14 professors and masters employed in the several departments of this college, and from 130 to 150 students.—There is a line of railway from C. S to Reigate and Godstonegreen, and N to Wandsworth, whence there is a navigable communication with the river Thames: horses are the motive power employed on this railroad. The London and Southampton railway crosses this railway between Wandsworth and Mitcham. The C. and London railway, from C. to the station in Tooleystreet near London-bridge, is considered to be a main trunk line, connecting London, by its junction with the Dover and Brighton railway, with the counties of Kent, Surrey, and Sussex, and with the S coast. from Deal and Dover to Brighton and Worthing. There is also a railway between C. and Epsom, which was originally worked by atmospheric pressure.

CROYLAND, or Crowland, an ancient town and p. in the co. of Lincoln, 47 m. SSE of Lincoln, and 14 m. E by N of Stamford, on the E bank of the Welland. The town of Croyland consists principally of four streets, separated by water-courses bordered by willows, which give it a rural and at the same time a romantic aspect. The streets are united by a triangular bridge extending over the Welland, the Nen, and a capacious drain called Catwater. The workmanship of this bridge—which is of the age of Edward II.—has been much admired. It consists of 3 segments of a circle meeting in a central point. This town was at one time surrounded by impassable marshes, which have now been drained and converted into rich meadows and luxuriant corn-fields. Area of p. 29,070 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,795; in 1831, 2,716; in 1841, 2,973. The first great school established in England after the Norman conquest, was founded at C., at the close of the 11th cent. The following was the course of education:- "Early in the morning the young pupils heard the lectures of Brother Odo in grammar. At noon Terricus, a sophist, explained to those of advanced years the logic of Aristotle, with the introduction and comments of Porphyrius and Averroës. Brother William was occupied in the afternoon by an exposition of the rhetoric of Cicero and Quinctilian. On Sundays and holidays Gislebert preached in the neighbouring churches, and chiefly directed his arguments against the errors and infidelity of the Jews." The inhabitants of C. are principally occupied in dairying, and in rearing geese, and fowling, and fishing,-for the privilege of pursuing the two latter occupations, they pay to the Crown, as they originally did to the abbot of Croyland, £300 per annum. C. is peculiarly interesting from its great antiquity, its singular bridge, and the ruins of its splendid and extensive abbey.
CROZANT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Creuse, cant. of Dun-le-Palleteau. Pop. 1,214.

CROZET ISLANDS, a group in the S Pacific, in S lat. 47° 30', E long. 46° to 48°. They are all volcanic, of a wild and rugged aspect, and nearly inaccessible. The mountains rise in peaks and cones to an elevation of from 4,000 to 5,000 ft., and are crowned with perpetual snow, while dense fogs frequently envelop their bases.

CROZON, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Finistère, arrond. of Chateaulin.—The cant. comprises 7 com. Pop. 15,600.—The town is 18 m. WNW of Chateaulin. Pop. 8,464. It has a small harbour.—Also a com. in the dep. of Indre, cant. of Aigurande. Pop. 1,047.
CRUACHAN. See BENCRUACHAN.

CRUACH-LUSSA, or CRUACH-LUSACH, that is, 'the Mountain of plants,' a mountain in the district of Knapdale, Argyleshire. It stretches over a great extent of country, being about 8 m. broad at the base. It is supposed to attain an alt. of 3,000 ft. above the level of the sea. From its summit, in a clear day, a fine view may be obtained of Islay, Jura, and other islands of the Hebrides, and of the island of Rathlin off the Irish coast. CRUAGH, CREAGH, or CREVAGH, a parish in co. Dublin, 6 m. S of Dublin. Area 4,460 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,216; in 1841, 979.

CRUCES, a town of New Grenada, in the dep. of Ystmo, on the l. bank of the Chagres river, a little above the junction of the Ovispo, 23 m. direct dis- 102. tance from the Atlantic, and 44 m. by the windings of the river. The Chagres has here a current of about 3} m. per hour, which it seldom exceeds even in the rainy season. It has been known, however, to rise 40 ft. above its level, and submerge the greater part of the town. C. is 8 hours' journey on muleback from Panama. See CHAGRES.
CRUCES (Los), a town of Mexico, in the prov. and 115 m. N of Durango, on an affluent of the Nasos.
CRUCIFER, a village in Henderson co., in the state of Tennessee, 126 m. WSW of Nashville.
CRUDEN, a parish in the district of Ellon, in Aberdeenshire, extending 8 m. along the coast. Pop. in 1801, 1,934; in 1841, 2,349.
CRUDWELL, a parish in Wiltshire, 4 m. NE of Malmsbury. Area 4,820 acres. Pop. 681.
CRUE'JOULS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Laissac. Pop. 1,589.
CRUET, a village of Savoy, 10 m. SE of Chambery. Pop. 1,113.
CRUGGION, a township in the p. of Abberbury, Montgomeryshire, 61 m. NE by N of Welshpool, bounded W and N by the river Severn. Pop. 171.
CRUISETOWN, a parish in co. Meath, 13 m. SW of Nobber. Area 1,863 acres. Pop. in 1831, 427; in 1841, 432.
CRUIT, an island in co. Donegal, 4 m. N by W of Dunglo. It is separated from the mainland of the Rosses by a sound about 1 m. broad, and it measures nearly 2 m. in length. II. ~ CRU
CRULUI, a commune of France, in the dep. of Orne, cant. of Laigle. Pop. 1,012.

CRUMLIN, a small market and post-town in the p. of Camlin, co. Antrim, 53 m. S of Antrim, on the Camlin river, at the mouth of which is a wharf, commanding the navigation of Lough Neagh and its canals. There are extensive flour-mills on the Camlin. Pop. in 1831, 643; in 1841, 568.—Also a parish in co. Dublin, 21 m. SW of Dublin. Pop. in 1831, 958; in 1841, 1,024.—Also a village in the latter p. Pop. in 1831, 544; in 1841, 258. In 1690, King William encamped here with his army, settled the method of granting protections, and issued his proclamation for depreciating the brass currency of James II. Pop. in 1831, 544; in 1841, 268.

CRUMMOCK WATER, a lake of Cumberland, 3 m. below Buttermere, between the mountain-ridges of Melbreak on the W, and Grasmoor on the E. It is nearly 4 m. long and 2 m. broad, and contains 3 little islands. The principal object of notice is Scaleforce cascade, in the glen betwixt Melbreak and Blea Crag. The fall is one clear leap of 156 ft. with a smaller fall below.
CRUMSALL, or CRUMPSALL, a township in Manchester p., Lancashire, 3 m. N of Manchester, on the river Ink. Area 660 acres. Pop. 2,745.
CRUNDAL, a parish in the co. of Kent, 8 m. SSW of Canterbury, E of the river Stour. Area 1,530 acres. Pop. 272.
CRUNWEAR, a parish in Pembrokeshire, 6 m. ESE of Narberth. Pop. 282.
CRUPET, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. and 9 m. SE of Namur. Pop. 562.
CRUSHEEN, a village in the p. of Inchicronane, co. Clare, 7 m. NNE of Ennis. Pop. 194.
CRUSINALLO, a village of the Sardinian states, in the prov. and 6 m. WSW of Pallanza. Pop. 200.
CRUSSILLES, a town of the Sardinian states, in the prov. of Carouge. Pop. 1,500.
CRUWYS-MORCHARD, a parish in Devonshire, 5\frac{1}{4} m. W of Tiverton. Area 6,290 acres. Pop. 670.
CRUX-EASTON, a parish in Southamptonshire, 6 m. NNW of Whitchurch. Area 950 acres. Pop.
CRUX-LA-VILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nièvre, cant. of Saint-Saulge. Pop. 2,016.
CRUYBEKE, a commune and town of Belgium, in E Flanders, 12 m. NE of Dendermonde. Pop. 2,704. About 130,000 pairs of sabots, or wooden shoes, are annually made here.
CRUYSHANTEM, a commune and town of Belgium, in E Flanders, 16 m. SW of Gand. Pop. 6,742.

CRUZ, a lake of Brazil, in the prov. of Santa- Catharina, district of São-Francisco. It is 6 m. in length, and of considerable depth; and is intersected at right angles by the Itapicu, which thus gives it the cross-form from which its name is derived.

CRUZ (LA), a mountain in Patagonia, in S lat. 53° 40′ 45", W long. 72° 04′ 00". It is steep, and rises abruptly from the sea, to an alt. of 2,280 ft. The officers of the Beagle found upon its summit, in the month of January 1827, a glass bottle containing a roll of papers left by Cordova in 1789.
CRUZ (LA), a town of New Grenada, in the intendancy of Cauca, prov. and 10 m. ESE of Buenaventura, on the S bank of the Dagua, 18 m. above the entrance of that river into the bay of Choco.

CRUZ (CABO DE), a promontory of the island of Cuba, on the S coast, to the S of the Bayo-de-Bueno- Esperanza, and forming the SW extremity of the Sierra Tarquino, in N lat. 19° 50' 11", and W long. 76° 44′ 50". It is commonly regarded as the extreme S point of the island of Cuba; but Punta-del- Ingles, or English point, which lies about 4 m. to the E of it in 19° 48' 30", is the most southern point. 3 B
CRUZ (Punta de LA), a headland forming the W side of the bay of Santa C., on the coast of Venezuela, 4 m. W by S of Punta Comona.

CRUZ (SANTA), or SAINTE CROIX, one of the Danish West Indies, situated at the mouth of the Caribbean sea, in 18° 48' N lat., and 64° 48' W long. It is of an oval form, about 30 m. in length from E to W, and 5 m. in extreme breadth. Its area is estimated at 81 sq. m. It is inferior to St. Thomas in its maritime commerce, but of greater importance in area, fertility, products, and internal resources. With the exception of a few hills in the neighbourhood of the cap., which reach an alt. of 1,400 ft., the whole island is nearly level; and about two-thirds is fit for raising sugar. Its surface was divided in 1754 into equal portions of 150 acres each; which division is still retained, and, with different shades of culture, gives a varied aspect to its area. The roads are good, and average from 24 to 30 ft. wide; running in straight lines through the island, and at right angles. The soil is not very rich, but tolerably fertile, yet owing to droughts the crops are uncertain. Christianstadt, the capital, is extremely well-built-its houses are of stone, and commodious; but Fredstoed or West-end is the principal commercial town. The government of the island is under a governor-general, whose jurisdiction extends to the other Danish colonies in these seas, and who is assisted by 2 councillors: there is also 'the Burgher council,' consisting of 7 members. A force of about 200 men compose the garrison. The code of Christian V., together with the rescripts of the Crown, constitute the law. A number of the largest estates in the island are the property of British subjects; and the pop. is essentially English in its language and habits. About one-third of the slaves belong to the king of Denmark, as owner or mortgagee of the estates; but the slaves are in the course of annual and gradual emancipation, as is the case in the other Danish islands. Pop. in 1841, 3,200 whites, and 20,000 slaves. In 1816 this island produced upwards of 40,000 hogsheads of sugar; but in seasons less favourable its crop is not more than 10,000 or 12,000 hds. The cultivation of coffee, indigo, and cotton has been generally abandoned on this island for many years. The average value of sugar raised is about 1,200,000 rix-dollars; of rum, 500,000 r.-d. About 150 cwt. of cotton is annually grown. The competition of Porto-Rico and the Brazils, in sugar, has greatly crippled the commerce of this island. In 1800 it produced above 40,000 hds. of sugar, and 30,000 puncheons of rum. -This island was discovered by Columbus in 1493; and first settled by the Dutch and English in 1625. In 1649 the Dutch were expelled by the English. In 1650 the English were routed by the Spaniards from Porto-Rico, who laid the island waste, but abandoned it to the French in the course of the next year. In 1733, the French Crown sold its rights to a Danish company for 1,611,000 rix-dollars, or £75,000. From this company it was subsequently purchased by the king of Denmark. In 1801 it was taken by the English. It was restored in the following year, but recaptured in 1807; and it remained under the dominion of Britain till 1815, when it was again ceded to the Danes.

CRUZ (SANTA), a town of the Azores, on the NE coast of the island of Flores, 7 m. NNE of Lagens. -Also a town on the NE coast of the island of Graciosa, in the same group.—Also a town on the S coast of the island of Pico in the same group, with a port named Calhetta.
